Q1: Honestly, my study routine was pretty chaotic. I'd tell myself I'd study at certain times, but then I'd just... not. I spent a lot of hours with my materials open, maybe around 32 hours a week technically, but a lot of that was me staring at screens without really absorbing much. I'm a visual learner so I'd watch videos and look at diagrams, but organizing an actual routine felt overwhelming most days.
Q2: My pattern was all over the place. Some weeks I'd be somewhat consistent, but then deadlines would hit or I'd have extracurricular stuff, and everything would fall apart. I'd end up cramming or just doing the bare minimum to get by. The anxiety of not knowing if I was doing enough made it hard to stick to any schedule.
Q3: Usually it was panic, to be honest. I'd suddenly remember an assignment was due or I'd get a notification about something. Sometimes I logged in just to check if I'd missed anything important, which happened more often than I'd like to admit. The peer support group I was part of would sometimes remind me too, which helped.
Q4: Not really, no. It never felt natural or automatic. There were brief periods where I'd check in more regularly, usually when my peers were active in discussions and I felt like I should contribute. But that routine never stuck. It always felt like something I had to force myself to do rather than something that just happened.
Q5: The videos were definitely most useful for me since I learn better visually. I watched quite a bit of video content, almost 300 minutes worth. The discussion boards were helpful too because my peers would explain things in different ways that sometimes clicked better than the lectures. When someone broke down a concept with examples, that really helped.
Q6: The readings and text-heavy resources I mostly ignored or skimmed at best. My assignment completion was pretty low because I'd look at them, feel overwhelmed, and then just... not do them. I kept telling myself I'd get to them later. The longer documents especially felt like too much when I was already anxious about everything else.
Q7: If I could see immediate relevance to the exam or if it was explained visually, it felt worth my time. Also, activities where my peers were engaged made me feel like I should participate too. But if something felt abstract or disconnected from what I needed to pass, I'd drift away from it pretty quickly.
Q8: Optional activities felt like luxuries I couldn't afford mentally. Required ones at least had some external pressure pushing me, even if I still procrastinated. With optional stuff, there was always this voice saying 'you should focus on what's mandatory first,' and then I'd never circle back. The anxiety made prioritizing really difficult.
Q9: Yeah, definitely. I'd download lecture slides and videos to my laptop and study from there without being online. I also used some YouTube videos that explained concepts better than the course materials. My resources are limited so I'd go to the library sometimes and use their computers or textbooks there instead of buying my own.
Q10: A lot of my studying happened with downloaded PDFs and screenshots of diagrams I'd saved. I'd sit with those for hours, trying to make sense of things, but none of that showed up anywhere. Also, talking through concepts with friends from my peer support group in person wouldn't register on any system. Those conversations helped more than the LMS knows.
Q11: All the time. I'd watch a video once online, then download it or take screenshots of key frames and study from those repeatedly. My internet isn't always reliable at home, so offline access was kind of necessary. The LMS would only show that one initial view even though I might have reviewed the material five more times.
Q12: There were definitely low points, usually when multiple things piled up at once. I'd feel this wave of sadness and just couldn't bring myself to engage. My anxiety would spike thinking about everything I was behind on, which ironically made me avoid the LMS even more. It became this cycle I couldn't break out of easily.
Q13: Absolutely. When I was tired or had too many competing things going on, the LMS was the first thing I'd neglect. Fatigue made it hard to focus on videos even though that's my preferred way to learn. Confusion about what was actually required versus optional also made me waste time figuring things out instead of actually learning.
Q14: More times than I can count. I'd have videos playing while my mind was somewhere else entirely, worrying about other things. I'd scroll through discussion posts without really reading them. My eyes were on the screen but I wasn't present. It was like going through the motions without actually being there mentally.
Q15: The assignments especially. I'd know they were important but the anxiety of potentially failing made me avoid starting them. It sounds backwards but starting felt like admitting I might not be able to do it. So I'd watch more videos instead, telling myself I was preparing, when really I was just avoiding the harder work.
Q16: Deadlines were basically the only thing that made me actually submit anything. I'd have this burst of panicked activity right before something was due. My LMS logins probably spiked around deadline times. Without that external pressure, I'd just drift along watching videos and participating in discussions without producing actual work.
Q17: When I got feedback, it was mixed. Positive feedback made me feel briefly okay, but negative feedback or low scores would send me into a spiral. I'd avoid checking grades sometimes because I didn't want to face it. The peer support helped me process feedback better, but it still affected my willingness to engage afterward.
Q18: The course having 11 different online components felt overwhelming. I never quite knew where to focus my energy. The structure felt loose in some ways, which should have been freeing but actually made my anxiety worse because I wasn't sure if I was doing enough. More guidance on priorities would have helped me a lot.
Q19: The video content being available was great for my learning style. But the sheer number of resources and lack of clear pathways made engagement harder. I'd get lost trying to figure out what to do next. The discussion forums were good because peers helped clarify things, but navigating everything alone was tough.
Q20: That's basically me, isn't it? I think it's about anxiety and avoidance. You can spend hours watching videos, reading discussions, trying to understand things, but when it comes to actually producing work, there's this wall. The activity shows you're trying, but the submissions show where the fear wins out.
Q21: I can see that happening if someone studies mostly offline or already knows the material. For me, my decent exam score came from cramming and visual memory, not from consistent LMS engagement. Sometimes understanding comes from sources outside the system, and the grades reflect knowledge the LMS never saw you acquire.
Q22: Logs can show when you logged in and what you clicked on, but they can't show if you actually understood anything or if you were just anxiously scrolling. They miss the offline studying, the peer conversations, the mental state you were in. A sad student staring at a screen looks the same as an engaged one in the data.
Q23: Being engaged means actually caring about and connecting with what you're learning, not just going through motions. It's when the material sticks and you want to explore it further. For me, real engagement happened in spurts, usually during peer discussions or when a video really clicked. It's more about mental presence than physical activity.
Q24: The sadness and anxiety that colored everything I did wouldn't show up at all. The peer support conversations that kept me from giving up completely aren't tracked. My financial stress affecting my access to resources, my offline studying, the times I genuinely tried but couldn't follow through—none of that appears in any log. The data shows a drifter, but it doesn't show why.
